smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: made these for you
thread.body_text:
hi Ellie, spent a few minutes on the Twisted Pepper site and I'm a little annoyed we're not working together yet haha.

but that's not why I reached out. I handle Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of sending a BS pitch, I built you some.

built 3 ad concepts from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and customer reviews. it's all done and waiting on my side.

should I send it over?

- Kyle

thread.subject: RE: made these for you
thread.body_text:
Ellie - the thing most brands notice first isn't the ads, it's the brand playbook. it maps your voice, the exact words your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's ready to go with the mockups.

 want me to pass both along?

- Kyle
please send me the brand playbook please

here you go Ellie - 3 ad mockups built from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus your brand playbook .

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Twisted Pepper is a founder-run gourmet dip and soup mix brand with a low order value and no ads running, so lead with a bundle-first test to prove Meta can work before we talk scale.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A handcrafted gourmet food brand started by Ellie Hughes in 2005. She sells dip mixes, soup mixes, BBQ rubs and seasonings direct to shoppers and to wholesale. It runs on Shopify.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: 103 products from $5.50 to $48.99. Most single items sit around $6 to $13. They also sell 3-packs and 4-packs that run $22.99 to $36, plus gift boxes. Everything is gluten free, low sodium, no MSG.
deep_dive.positioning: Approachable gourmet. Fine-restaurant flavor made fast and easy for busy home cooks. Not mass-market bland, not fussy either.
deep_dive.who_buys: Busy home cooks who want bold flavor with little effort, and gift-givers buying for the food lover in their life. Free shipping over $40 and gift boxes point to both.
customer_voice: I am absolutely addicted to your soups and dips/cheese ball mixes. To be honest - all those I bought from you never went for gifts! They went in our stomachs, and to share with extended family and friends over the holidays. They were a huge hit with everyone, so I will be restocking my hidden stash and sharing with friends.
My husband Loves the Pig Sweat Rub. He sprinkles on his bacon while cooking, and anything pork! I order 4 jars at time. FYI - He also loves the Outlaws Season All.
strengths: Real founder story going back to 2005, with a clear voice and strong repeat-buyer love in the testimonials.
Ready-made bundles and gift boxes that push order value up past the single-item price, which is what paid ads need.
gaps: Almost no reviews live on product pages. The homepage even still has placeholder review text. Very little public social proof to lean on in ads.
No Meta ads found running, so we start from zero. No pixel history or past winners to build on, and the shipping policy page just says Coming Soon.

game_plan.headline: Start with the bundles, not the $6 mixes, and prove one ad can sell before we spend real money.

phases.goal: Prove that a Twisted Pepper ad can turn a cold shopper into a buyer at all. Learn which of the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads people respond to.
campaigns: One sales campaign, small budget, testing the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads against cold shoppers.
A simple retargeting ad for people who visit the site but do not buy.
lead_products: Lead with the 4 Pack Dip Mixes at $22.99 (Veggies & Chiles, I Love Onions, Mild, I Like it Spicy, or Dipping Madness). At $22.99 they clear the $40 free shipping threshold faster than a $6 single mix, so the order is worth enough to carry ad cost. Dips are also their most repeat-loved product based on the testimonials.

Run the first Scroll-Stopper ad that features a dip or 4-pack. We go live with that one first because dips have the strongest customer love in their own reviews and the bundle price makes the math work.
phases.creative: Use the 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet. Lean on the founder voice and the words their own buyers use, like 'addicted to your dips' and 'Get Creative, make it your own'. Show the party roll-up and cheeseball uses, not just a bag on a table.
phases.budget: Start around $50 a day. Enough to learn which ad works without betting big before we know anything.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One of the 3 ads clearly beats the others, and we get enough sales to see a pattern. Not profit yet. Proof.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the winning ad and widen who sees it.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad from month one with a higher budget.
Test 2 or 3 new versions of the winner to keep it fresh.
Widen the cold audience beyond the first test group.
lead_products: Keep the winning bundle as the hero. Add a gift-angle test using the Gift Boxes and 3-pack Soup Collections ($30), since gifting showed up strongly in their reviews.
phases.creative: Build fresh ads off whatever angle won. If the 'easy party dip' angle won, make more of that. Start collecting and using real customer quotes in the ads.
phases.budget: Step up toward $100 to $150 a day, only if month one showed a working ad. If it did not, we fix the offer first, not the budget.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad holds up at higher spend and starts paying for itself, not just breaking even.

phases.goal: Scale what works and add a second product line so we are not resting on one bundle.
campaigns: Scale the proven ad and audience.
Launch a second product line: BBQ Rubs and Seasonings.
Push retargeting harder around the free shipping over $40 offer to lift order size.
lead_products: Bring in the BBQ Rubs (Uptown BBQ Rub $10.49, Pig Sweat, Outlaws Season All). Their reviews show a devoted rub buyer who reorders 4 jars at a time, so there is real repeat demand to tap. Pair rubs into a set to hit the $40 threshold.
phases.creative: Fresh ads for the rub line using the same proven format. Keep the dip winner running underneath.
phases.budget: Scale in steps only as long as the return holds. Never jump the budget faster than the results allow.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two product lines both selling from ads, a repeatable process, and a clear picture of what each new dollar returns.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: enough to get roughly 25 to 40 clicks a day at typical food-ecom click costs, which is the floor for learning which ad works without wasting money

estimates.label: Order value we are planning around
estimates.value: About $10 per order as a starting guess, which is why we push bundles
estimates.basis: their catalog median price is $9.99 and we used that as a stand-in for average order value since we cannot see their real Shopify data. This is exactly why we lead with the $22.99 4-packs, to lift it toward the $40 free shipping line.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We are not putting a dollar figure on this yet
estimates.basis: we cannot see their current sales, spend, or return on ads. Guessing a number could be embarrassingly wrong on the call. Once we know their real order value and margin, we set a target. Until then the honest answer is: month one proves it works, months two and three grow it.

assumptions: Their real average order value is low and close to the catalog median, so bundles must do the heavy lifting.
They have healthy enough margin on a $22.99 bundle to afford paid ads. We must confirm this on the call.
The Shopify checkout and shipping actually work, even though the shipping policy page says Coming Soon.
Ellie can supply real product photos and is open to gathering customer reviews we can use in ads.

questions.question: Are you spending anything on ads right now, on Meta or anywhere else?
questions.listening_for: We found no ads running. If she says zero, we are truly starting from scratch and should set expectations for a learning month. If she stopped ads before, ask why.

questions.question: Who handles your marketing today, you or someone else?
questions.listening_for: If it is all Ellie, she is stretched thin and wants it off her plate. That is our opening. If she has someone, find out what they have tried.

questions.question: Have you run Meta ads before, and what happened?
questions.listening_for: Past attempts tell us if the problem was creative, offer, or the low order value. Listen for 'it didn't make money' and dig into why.

questions.question: On a $22.99 4-pack, roughly how much is left after the cost to make and ship it?
questions.listening_for: This is the make-or-break number. If there is little margin on a $10 to $23 order, paid ads will struggle. We need enough room to pay for the click and still profit.

questions.question: What is your actual average order value, and what pushes it up, the bundles or the free shipping offer?
questions.listening_for: We are guessing $10 from the median price. Her real number changes the whole plan. If it is already near $40, ads get much easier and we can lead differently.

questions.question: Which single product sells the most, and which do people reorder?
questions.listening_for: Reviews point to dips and BBQ rubs. If she confirms a clear bestseller, we lead the first ad with that instead of guessing.

questions.question: Do you have real customer reviews saved anywhere, even in email, that we could show in ads?
questions.listening_for: The site shows almost none. If she has reviews sitting unused, that fixes our biggest gap fast. If she has none at all, we need a plan to collect them.

questions.question: Do you have good product photos or short videos, and can you make more?
questions.listening_for: Ads live or die on the picture. If she only has plain bag shots, we need lifestyle images of the dips and party spreads that her reviews describe.

questions.question: Is your checkout and shipping fully working? Your shipping policy page online just says Coming Soon.
questions.listening_for: A broken or unclear checkout kills ad money. Confirm orders actually flow and shipping cost is clear at checkout.

questions.question: How much of your business comes from wholesale and fundraisers versus your website?
questions.listening_for: She has both. If wholesale is the real engine, paid ads to shoppers may matter less, and we tailor the plan. Do not assume DTC is the priority.

questions.question: When people find you online, are they searching for things like 'gluten free dip mix' or 'BBQ rub', or do they only find you by name?
questions.listening_for: People do search for dip mixes, soup mixes, and BBQ rubs by category. That is a real Google opening later. Do not push it in month one, but flag it if she says people search for these terms.

objections.objection: My products only cost $6 to $10. There is no way ads make money on that.
objections.answer: You are right that a $6 mix alone will not carry an ad. That is exactly why we do not lead with it. We lead with your $22.99 4-packs and gift boxes, and lean on your free shipping over $40 offer to nudge order size up. We prove the math on the bundle first, then add products.

objections.objection: I have never run ads and I do not have a big budget to burn.
objections.answer: Good. We start at about $50 a day on purpose. Month one is not about scale, it is about proving one ad can sell before you spend real money. If it does not work, you have not bet the farm, and we fix the offer before we spend more.

objections.objection: I do not have many reviews or fancy photos.
objections.answer: That is the honest gap, and it is fixable. You already have glowing customer notes, like the person addicted to your dips and the husband who buys four jars of rub at a time. We put those words to work in the ads and set up a simple way to collect more as orders come in.

objections.objection: I already sell fine through wholesale and events, why bother with ads?
objections.answer: Then we treat ads as a second channel that you own, not something that replaces what works. Wholesale depends on other people's shelves. Ads build your own list of repeat buyers who reorder, like the ones already in your reviews.

red_flags.flag: Order value looks low. The catalog median is $9.99 and single mixes run $6.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Paid ads need enough profit per order to cover the click. A $6 order almost never can. The whole plan hinges on bundles and the $40 threshold lifting this, and on her margin being healthy. Confirm before promising anything.

red_flags.flag: Almost no reviews live on the site, and the homepage still shows placeholder review text.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold shoppers trust other shoppers. With no visible social proof, the first ads work harder. We must pull her existing customer notes into the ads and start collecting reviews early.

red_flags.flag: No Meta ads found running and no ad history to build on.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We start from zero with no past winners and likely a cold pixel. Month one is genuinely a learning period. Do not let Ellie expect fast profit.

red_flags.flag: Shipping policy page online says Coming Soon.
red_flags.why_it_matters: If shipping cost or timing is unclear at checkout, people who click the ad may abandon. Confirm shipping and checkout actually work before spending.

red_flags.flag: Huge catalog of 103 products with heavy overlap.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Too many choices can confuse both shoppers and ad targeting. We must pick a few clear heroes, not try to sell everything. This is a strength if we focus and a trap if we do not.

other_channel_openings: Google Search is a real opening for later. People actively search for 'gluten free dip mix', 'dry soup mix', and 'BBQ rub', which are categories she already sells. Worth raising once Meta is proven, not in month one.

SEO on their recipe pages. They already publish recipes like Spinach Artichoke Hot Dip and Lentil Soup. Recipe content that ranks can pull in free traffic that ties straight to the products. A slower play, but a genuine fit given they already do it.
